figures.caption: Figure 3.1: Representative two-dimensional positive-curvature output from the AInstein atlas model. The (x, y) directions are the 2d coordinates of the respective patch, and the z direction is the value of that tensor component. The close agreement between metric and Ricci components corroborates the strong learning of the Ric(g) = g condition directly.

Figure 3.2: This PINN architecture combines a global conformal-factor ansatz with a global optimisation diagnostic. The loss plot shows the same empirical separation between known realisable and obstructed cases that is later used to assess unresolved prescribers.

Figure 3.5: Five snapshots from the genus-1 learning process, taken from the embedding sequence reported in [19]. The optimisation rapidly lowers the Willmore energy, converging to the Clifford-torus minimum.
Figure 3.4: Neural Willmore flow outputs from [19]. The first row shows the final learned embeddings for genuses 0, 1, and 2; the second row shows the corresponding Willmore-losses over training.

Figure 2.1: Two recurrent design patterns for geometric PINNs. Atlas models trade a more local representation for explicit transition losses, while embedding models avoid overlap terms when a suitable global realisation is available.

Figure 3.3: MDS embeddings of learned metrics for three spherical-harmonic prescribers. The first two are benchmark cases with known realisability, while the third illustrates the geometry of the case Y3,2 with conjectured realisability.
